This process occurs automatically through the interactions of individual buyers and sellers, coordinating resource allocation without explicit government intervention. Economists frequently refer to this emergent order as "the invisible hand of the market." By responding to price signals, participants optimize outcomes, ensuring that resources flow toward their highest value uses.<|im_end|>

Prices in competitive markets are primarily shaped by the dynamic interaction between supply and demand. When producers offer more goods than consumers desire at a specific price level, the surplus drives prices downward. Conversely, scarcity relative to consumer needs pushes prices upward until equilibrium is reached.

This process occurs automatically through the interactions of individual buyers and sellers, coordinating resource allocation without explicit government intervention. Economists frequently refer to this emergent order as "the invisible hand of the market." By responding to price signals, participants optimize outcomes, ensuring that resources flow toward their highest value uses.<|im_end|>
